passages.explanation: The Kingdom of Heaven is like a king who made a wedding feast for his son. He sends servants to call those who are invited — they refuse to come. He sends more servants with details about the feast. They disregard it — one goes to his farm, another to his business. The rest seize the servants, treat them shamefully, and kill them. The king is furious, sends his armies, destroys those murderers, and burns their city. Then he tells his servants: the wedding is ready, but those invited weren't worthy. Go to the highways and invite as many as you find. The servants gather everyone — both bad and good — and the wedding is filled. But when the king comes in to see the guests, he sees a man without a wedding garment. He asks: friend, how did you come in without wedding clothes? The man is speechless. The king orders him bound and cast into outer darkness. Many are called, but few are chosen. The invitation is free and wide open — but it's not casual. You can't come to the feast and refuse to be changed by it.

The Pharisees take counsel to trap Jesus in his words. They send their disciples with the Herodians: teacher, we know you're truthful and teach God's way honestly. You don't defer to anyone. Tell us — is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar or not? Jesus, perceiving their wickedness: why do you test me, you hypocrites? Show me the tax coin. They bring a denarius. Whose image and inscription is this? Caesar's. Then give to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's. They marvel and leave him. If they join the Zealots in refusing taxes, they're arrested by Rome. If they endorse taxes, they lose the crowd. Jesus doesn't choose either side — he transcends the dilemma entirely.

That same day, Sadducees — who say there is no resurrection — come to him with a hypothetical: a woman marries seven brothers successively after each dies. In the resurrection, whose wife will she be? Jesus responds: you're mistaken, knowing neither the Scriptures nor the power of God. In the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like God's angels in heaven. Concerning the resurrection of the dead — haven't you read what was spoken to you by God: I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob? God is not the God of the dead but of the living. The multitudes are astonished. The Sadducees build a clever puzzle assuming resurrection is just a continuation of current life. Jesus says they've misunderstood both the afterlife and their own Scriptures.

When the Pharisees hear that Jesus silenced the Sadducees, they gather together. A lawyer among them tests him: teacher, which is the greatest commandment in the law? Jesus answers: you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind. This is the first and great commandment. A second is like it: you shall love your neighbour as yourself. The whole law and the prophets hang on these two commandments. Jesus reduces 613 commandments to two — and they're both about love. Every other command is a footnote to these.

While the Pharisees are gathered, Jesus asks them: what do you think about the Christ — whose son is he? They say: David's. He asks: how then does David in the Spirit call him Lord, saying — the Lord said to my Lord, sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet? If David calls him Lord, how is he his son? No one can answer him a word. From that day forward, no one dares ask him any more questions. Jesus doesn't just answer questions — he asks the one question that exposes the inadequacy of their entire messianic framework. The Messiah is David's son, yes — but also David's Lord. They can't explain it, so they stop asking.
